original_abstract: New memories rarely emerge in isolation; rather, they take shape in direct relation to established ones. This suggests that memory activism must go beyond simply surfacing occluded histories and actively work to destabilize the dominance of hegemonic narratives. This article explores this dynamic by examining how public monuments frequently become lightning rods for protest, particularly during periods of regime change where their destruction serves as a symbolic necessity. The inherent materiality of these monuments explains both their potential to cause profound offense and their unique utility as physical platforms for practicing dissent. Ultimately, the discussion surveys a range of strategies for recontextualizing existing landmarks to alter their inherited meanings and project alternative historical narratives into the public sphere.
humanized_abstract: New memories almost always link on to old ones; so memory activism, which always involves bringing suppressed or erased histories to light, will have to involve either disrupting or altering other memories and narratives that support the suppressed ones Memory activism: bringing the past to light and disrupting other pasts Explores how and why public memorials are an increasingly prominent site for protest, particularly where their physical destruction is necessary to signify a fundamental shift in power, political or otherwise. Analyzes both the structures of material offense and the material forms through which protest and the practice of dissent can be realized and played out. Also explores tactics of remembering and counter remembering by repurposing memorials to embed alternative meanings and narratives.

Analyzes both the structures of material offense and the material forms through which protest and the practice of dissent can be realized and played out. Also explores tactics of remembering and counter remembering by repurposing memorials to embed alternative meanings and narratives.
